reference: GATE CS — Programming and Data Structures / Non-Linear Data Structures

map.condition: Shortest path in weighted graph (non-negative weights)

formulas.name: Handshaking Lemma
formulas.latex: \sum_{v \in V} \deg(v) = 2|E|
formulas.plain: Sum of all degrees = 2 * |E|
formulas.whenToUse: Count edges from degree sequence or verify a graph exists
formulas.trap: In a directed graph, \sum \deg^+(v) = \sum \deg^-(v) = |E|, not 2|E|

formulas.name: Edges in Complete Graph K_n
formulas.whenToUse: Maximum possible edges in a simple undirected graph on n vertices
formulas.trap: For directed K_n the count is n(n-1) (each edge becomes two arcs)

formulas.name: Tree / Forest Edge Count
formulas.latex: \text{A tree on } n \text{ vertices has exactly } n-1 \text{ edges}
formulas.plain: Tree on n vertices => n-1 edges
formulas.whenToUse: Verify tree property or count spanning tree edges
formulas.trap: A forest with k trees on n vertices has n-k edges (not n-1)

formulas.name: Euler's Formula for Planar Graphs
formulas.latex: V - E + F = 2
formulas.plain: V - E + F = 2  (connected planar graph)
formulas.whenToUse: Prove a graph is non-planar or count faces in a planar embedding
formulas.trap: For a disconnected planar graph: V - E + F = 1 + C where C is the number of connected components

formulas.name: Planar Graph Edge Bound
formulas.latex: E \leq 3V - 6 \quad (V \geq 3)
formulas.plain: E <= 3V - 6 for simple planar graphs (V >= 3)
formulas.whenToUse: Quickly prove a dense graph (e.g. K_5) is non-planar
formulas.trap: Triangle-free planar graphs satisfy E <= 2V - 4 (tighter bound)

formulas.name: Chromatic Number Bounds
formulas.latex: \omega(G) \leq \chi(G) \leq \Delta(G) + 1
formulas.plain: clique_number <= chromatic_number <= max_degree + 1
formulas.whenToUse: Bound or determine the minimum colours needed to colour a graph
formulas.trap: Bipartite graphs (with at least one edge) always have chi(G) = 2, not Delta+1

formulas.name: BFS / DFS Time Complexity
formulas.latex: T_{\text{BFS}} = T_{\text{DFS}} = O(V + E) \text{ (adjacency list)}
formulas.plain: BFS = DFS = O(V + E) with adjacency list; O(V^2) with adjacency matrix
formulas.whenToUse: Analyse traversal cost; BFS for shortest path (unweighted), DFS for cycle/topo
formulas.trap: With an adjacency matrix the cost is O(V^2) regardless of the number of edges

formulas.name: BFS Shortest-Path (Unweighted)
formulas.latex: d(s, v) = \text{level of } v \text{ in BFS tree from } s
formulas.plain: BFS level of v from s = shortest hop-count distance d(s,v)
formulas.whenToUse: Find shortest (hop-count) path in an unweighted graph

terms.means: shortest-path distance from s to v

formulas.trap: BFS gives shortest path only for equal-weight (or unweighted) graphs; use Dijkstra for weights

formulas.name: DFS Back Edge & Cycle Detection
formulas.latex: \text{Undirected graph has cycle} \Leftrightarrow \text{DFS produces a back edge}
formulas.plain: Cycle in undirected graph <=> DFS back edge exists
formulas.whenToUse: Determine if an undirected graph is acyclic (is a forest)

terms.means: DFS edge connecting a vertex to an ancestor

formulas.trap: In directed graphs you also need to check for cross edges to detect cycles; back edges alone suffice for undirected graphs

formulas.name: Topological Sort (DAG)
formulas.latex: \text{Topological order exists} \Leftrightarrow G \text{ is a DAG}
formulas.plain: Topological sort exists iff G is a DAG (directed acyclic graph); DFS-based in O(V+E)
formulas.whenToUse: Schedule tasks with prerequisites or linearise a DAG
formulas.trap: Topological order is not unique unless the graph is a Hamiltonian path (each vertex has in-degree 1 or is the source)

formulas.name: Dijkstra's Algorithm Complexity
formulas.latex: O\!\left((V + E)\log V\right) \text{ with binary heap}
formulas.plain: Dijkstra: O((V+E) log V) with binary heap; O(V^2) with simple array
formulas.whenToUse: Single-source shortest path with non-negative edge weights
formulas.trap: Dijkstra FAILS with negative-weight edges; use Bellman-Ford instead

formulas.name: Bellman-Ford Algorithm
formulas.latex: T = O(VE); \text{ detects negative cycle if after } V-1 \text{ rounds relaxation still possible}
formulas.plain: Bellman-Ford: O(V*E); V-1 relaxation rounds; detects negative-weight cycles
formulas.whenToUse: Single-source shortest path allowing negative edges or detecting negative cycles
formulas.trap: Cannot use Dijkstra as a replacement when any edge weight is negative

formulas.name: Floyd-Warshall (All-Pairs Shortest Path)
formulas.plain: d[i][j] = min(d[i][j], d[i][k]+d[k][j]);  O(V^3) time, O(V^2) space
formulas.whenToUse: All-pairs shortest path; dense graphs; negative edges (no negative cycles)

terms.means: shortest path from i to j using only vertices 1..k as intermediates

formulas.trap: Floyd-Warshall runs in O(V^3) even for sparse graphs; prefer Dijkstra per source for sparse graphs

formulas.name: Kruskal's Algorithm
formulas.latex: T = O(E \log E) = O(E \log V) \text{ (sort edges + Union-Find)}
formulas.plain: Kruskal: sort edges O(E log E), then Union-Find; total O(E log V)
formulas.whenToUse: MST on sparse graphs; edge list is easier to sort
formulas.trap: Kruskal adds edges in sorted order and skips any edge that creates a cycle — it does NOT always add adjacent edges to the existing tree

formulas.name: Prim's Algorithm
formulas.latex: T = O(E \log V) \text{ with binary heap (adjacency list)}
formulas.plain: Prim: O(E log V) with binary heap; O(V^2) with adjacency matrix (good for dense graphs)
formulas.whenToUse: MST on dense graphs or when starting from a specific vertex
formulas.trap: Prim's with adjacency matrix is O(V^2), which beats Kruskal on dense (E = O(V^2)) graphs

formulas.name: MST Cut Property
formulas.latex: \text{The minimum-weight edge crossing any cut belongs to some MST}
formulas.plain: For any partition (S, V\S), the lightest crossing edge is in every MST (if weights distinct)
formulas.whenToUse: Prove an edge must or cannot be in the MST

terms.means: partition of V into two non-empty sets S and V\\S

formulas.trap: If the minimum crossing edge is NOT unique, there may be multiple MSTs that include different choices among those tied edges

formulas.name: Number of Spanning Trees (Kirchhoff's Theorem)
formulas.latex: \tau(G) = \det(L_k) \quad \text{(any cofactor of Laplacian } L = D - A\text{)}
formulas.plain: Number of spanning trees = any cofactor of the Laplacian matrix L = D - A
formulas.whenToUse: Count distinct spanning trees of a graph

terms.means: Laplacian with row k and column k deleted

formulas.trap: For K_n the number of spanning trees is n^{n-2} (Cayley's formula), not the Kirchhoff computation (though they agree)

formulas.name: Connected Components via DFS/BFS
formulas.latex: \text{# connected components} = \text{# DFS/BFS calls on fresh vertices}
formulas.plain: Run BFS/DFS; each new start = new component; O(V+E) total
formulas.whenToUse: Count or identify connected components in an undirected graph
formulas.trap: For directed graphs you need Strongly Connected Components (Kosaraju / Tarjan), not a simple DFS count

formulas.name: Strongly Connected Components — Kosaraju's
formulas.latex: T = O(V + E): \text{ DFS on } G \text{ (finish order)} \rightarrow \text{DFS on } G^T
formulas.plain: Kosaraju: 2 DFS passes (original then transpose graph); O(V+E)
formulas.whenToUse: Find all SCCs in a directed graph
formulas.trap: The second DFS must process vertices in REVERSE finish order from the first DFS

formulas.name: Bipartite Graph Condition
formulas.latex: G \text{ is bipartite} \Leftrightarrow G \text{ has no odd-length cycle} \Leftrightarrow \chi(G) \leq 2
formulas.plain: Bipartite <=> no odd cycle <=> 2-colourable; BFS 2-colouring check in O(V+E)
formulas.whenToUse: Check if a graph can be 2-coloured or partitioned into two independent sets
formulas.trap: An empty graph (no edges) is bipartite but has chi(G)=1, not 2

formulas.name: Euler Circuit Condition
formulas.latex: \text{Euler circuit exists} \Leftrightarrow G \text{ is connected and every vertex has even degree}
formulas.plain: Euler circuit: connected + all degrees even
formulas.whenToUse: Determine if a graph can be traversed along every edge exactly once (returning to start)

terms.means: number of edges incident to a vertex

formulas.trap: For directed graphs: Euler circuit requires in-degree = out-degree for every vertex AND the graph is strongly connected

formulas.name: Euler Path (Trail) Condition
formulas.latex: \text{Euler path exists} \Leftrightarrow G \text{ connected, exactly 2 vertices of odd degree}
formulas.plain: Euler path (not circuit): connected + exactly 2 odd-degree vertices (start and end)
formulas.whenToUse: Determine if every edge can be traversed exactly once without returning to the start

terms.means: vertex with an odd number of incident edges

formulas.trap: Zero odd-degree vertices => Euler circuit (stronger condition), not just Euler path

formulas.name: Hamiltonian Path / Cycle — NP-Complete
formulas.latex: \text{Hamiltonian Cycle} \in \text{NP-complete} \quad \text{(no known poly-time algorithm)}
formulas.plain: Hamiltonian cycle/path is NP-complete; no efficient general algorithm exists
formulas.whenToUse: Recognise that visiting every VERTEX exactly once is computationally hard

terms.means: no polynomial-time algorithm known unless P=NP

formulas.trap: Euler (every EDGE once) is solvable in O(E); Hamiltonian (every VERTEX once) is NP-complete — do not confuse them

formulas.name: Adjacency Matrix Space & Query
formulas.latex: \text{Space: } O(V^2); \quad \text{Edge query: } O(1)
formulas.plain: Adjacency matrix: O(V^2) space; O(1) edge existence query
formulas.whenToUse: Dense graphs or when fast edge-existence lookup is needed
formulas.trap: Adjacency matrix traversal (e.g. BFS) costs O(V^2) even if E << V^2

formulas.name: Adjacency List Space & Query
formulas.latex: \text{Space: } O(V + E); \quad \text{Edge query: } O(\deg(v))
formulas.plain: Adjacency list: O(V+E) space; edge query O(degree(v))
formulas.whenToUse: Sparse graphs; standard representation for BFS/DFS
formulas.trap: Edge existence check in an adjacency list is O(deg(v)), not O(1) like in a matrix

formulas.name: Graph Type Definitions
formulas.latex: \text{Simple} \subset \text{Multigraph} \subset \text{Pseudograph}; \quad \text{DAG}: \text{directed + acyclic}
formulas.plain: Simple graph: no self-loops, no multi-edges. Multigraph: allows multi-edges. Pseudograph: allows self-loops. DAG: directed acyclic graph.
formulas.whenToUse: Identify the class of graph in a problem statement
formulas.trap: A tree is a connected acyclic undirected graph; it is also a DAG if edges are directed consistently

formulas.name: Degree Sequence Realizability (Erdos-Gallai)
formulas.latex: \sum_{i=1}^{n} d_i \text{ must be even; check Erdos-Gallai conditions for simple graph}
formulas.plain: A sequence d1>=d2>=...>=dn is graphical iff sum is even and Erdos-Gallai inequalities hold
formulas.whenToUse: Determine whether a given degree sequence corresponds to a valid simple graph
formulas.trap: Even sum is necessary but not sufficient; e.g. (3,3,3,1) sums to 10 but fails Erdos-Gallai
